concerns.text: The plan proposes using the Ljung-Box test on PIT values to test for uniformity. Ljung-Box tests for autocorrelation in a time series, not uniformity of a distribution. A uniform distribution of PIT values is tested via Kolmogorov-Smirnov (KS) or Anderson-Darling tests against a Uniform(0,1) distribution. Using Ljung-Box fails to validate the core construct of 'distributional calibration' (US-2).

The plan states: 'Calculate P(y_true | model distribution)' for PIT. For ARIMA and Prophet, which output intervals but not full density functions, the plan does not specify how to derive the cumulative distribution function (CDF) required for PIT calculation. Without a defined CDF, the PIT metric is uncomputable, invalidating the construct validity of the 'Distributional Calibration' claim.

The bootstrap test resamples 'series' (1000 resamples). If the dataset contains ~100k series (M4), resampling 1000 series is statistically valid. However, if the plan falls back to UCI HAR (verified substitute) which has only ~10k samples or fewer series, the power of the bootstrap test to detect small calibration differences may be insufficient. The plan does not define a minimum number of series required to achieve statistical power for the bootstrap test.

The LSTM interval generation relies on 'Gaussian residual assumption'. If the true residuals are non-Gaussian (common in time series), the resulting intervals will be miscalibrated. The plan does not include a diagnostic step to validate the Gaussian assumption before computing PIT/CRPS, risking a false negative on calibration quality (i.e., the model appears miscalibrated due to wrong interval construction, not poor forecasting).
